turns.text: Hello Bula, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're adjusting to life after having your baby, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thank you for asking. It feels... honestly, it feels a bit heavy to even start unpacking it, especially while the house is this quiet. Six weeks out, I’m still finding my footing.

I went into this wanting a partnership, you know? I’d read all the books, I had my notes, and I was so determined to view this as a natural transition, not a medical emergency. But living out here in Halifax, the distance from the clinic felt like a physical weight even before I started labor. When I’d walk into those appointments, I could feel the clock ticking. The providers would start moving down their checklist—routine this, standard protocol that—and I felt like I was fighting just to be heard as a person. 

I remember one afternoon, my heart was literally pounding in my chest because I had to interrupt a doctor who hadn't even looked me in the eye yet. I wasn't trying to be "difficult," but I just needed them to see me. I felt like a set of vitals they needed to record rather than a woman about to become a mother. It was exhausting. I’m proud I stuck to my values, but I’m left wondering why it had to be such an uphill battle just to keep my agency intact.
When you think about your goals for recovery and support in the days and weeks after birth, what did you most want or need — and how well did the postnatal care you received address those needs?

Honestly? I wanted someone to look at me, not just the baby. In the hospital, the focus was entirely on the clinical "success" of the birth—the vitals, the recovery markers—and then I was sent home to the silence of the woods.

Postpartum has been the loneliest part. I needed someone to acknowledge that my transition was emotional and systemic, not just physical. I wanted a provider who understood that the "low-risk" label on my chart didn't mean my mental health or my isolation wasn't a factor. I needed a bridge, someone to check in on the *person* living in this quiet house, not just someone to ask if my stitches were healing or if the baby was gaining weight. 

My care felt like it ended at the clinical finish line. There was no real transition plan for the isolation. I think if they had spent even five minutes asking how I was handling the solitude, or validating the fact that I’m doing this on my own—without a partner there to lean on—I would have felt so much less invisible. Instead, I left feeling like I’d checked their boxes, but my own needs for support were left entirely unaddressed. It’s been a hard realization, feeling like my care was just a series of clinical clearances.
What did 'good' postnatal care look like in your mind before you gave birth — and how did that compare to what you actually experienced?

Before the baby arrived, "good" care felt like an extension of the partnership I’d fought for during pregnancy. I imagined a team that would offer a soft place to land—someone who recognized that the "postpartum" period isn't just about uterine involution or feeding charts. I envisioned follow-ups where we might talk about the hormonal shift, the identity crisis of becoming a mother, and the reality of managing all of this in a rural home, away from a ready-made village. I expected a holistic check-in where my intuition as a new mom was treated as a valid data point.

In reality? It felt like a drive-through. My appointments were fast, transactional, and laser-focused on the baby’s growth and my physical healing. It felt like once I was "cleared" by the medical standards, the healthcare system effectively washed its hands of me. 

There was no conversation about the weight of the silence here, or the fact that being an unmarried mother in a rural town adds a layer of quiet, logistical stress that nobody seems to account for. I felt like a patient who had "performed well" by having no complications, so the system assumed I didn't need support. It turns out, being "low-risk" clinically just made me invisible when I actually needed the most care.
When you think about feeding your baby — whether through breastfeeding, formula, or a combination — what motivated your choices, and how supported did you feel in pursuing the approach you wanted?

Feeding felt like the ultimate test of my resolve. I felt so much pressure to do it "right," but I wanted it to be an intuitive, natural extension of the bond I was building. I chose to breastfeed because it felt like the most direct way to keep that connection going, especially when the isolation here started to feel suffocating. It was one of the few things that made me feel like I was still *me*, you know?

But the support? It was… lacking. In the hospital, it felt like a series of quick, aggressive demonstrations rather than a conversation. It was all about latch efficiency and technical mechanics. Every time I asked a question about my own comfort or the emotional aspect of it, the responses felt sterile. They were so focused on the clinical "success" of the feeding that they missed the frustration I was experiencing. 

When I got home, that lack of support magnified. Every time it got difficult—every time I was tired or just crying on the couch in the middle of the night—I felt like I had to solve every issue myself. There was no one to call who wouldn't just quote me a manual. I didn't need a checklist; I needed someone to tell me it was okay to be exhausted and that I was doing a good job. I felt like I was navigating a minefield entirely on my own.

When you think about your emotional and mental health in the weeks and months after birth — the adjustment, the challenges, and the moments of wellbeing — what stands out most, and how well-supported did you feel?

The silence stands out the most. It’s a physical thing, really—that heavy, thick quiet of the woods when the sun goes down and it’s just me and the baby. There are moments of pure, overwhelming awe where I look at her and feel like I’ve discovered the center of the universe. But those moments are often followed by this sharp, cold ache of realizing I have no one to share that awe with.

I don't think the medical system accounts for the emotional tax of being "on" twenty-four-seven without a partner to tag out with. When I went for my checkups, I was terrified to admit that I was struggling mentally. I felt like if I spoke about the loneliness or the anxiety, I’d be labeled as "postpartum depressed" rather than just a woman in a very difficult, isolated life circumstance. I was scared they’d use my words as data against me, or push medication instead of looking at my environment.

I wanted support, but I felt I had to perform "wellness" to be taken seriously. I’ve had to be my own therapist, my own doula, and my own partner. It’s exhausting. I’m doing okay, I think, but I’m surviving rather than being cared for. I’ve realized that the system isn’t built to hold a woman’s whole, messy, beautiful reality—it’s built to monitor a body. And that feels like a failure.

When you think about the shift in your identity and sense of self after becoming a parent, what tensions or changes were most significant — and how did those changes interact with your other roles, such as partner, professional, or daughter?

The shift has been... total. It’s like who I was before—the professional, the independent woman—has been put into deep storage. My work life feels like a different universe. Before, my identity was so tied to my intellectual rigor and my independence. Now, my entire sense of self is filtered through the lens of being a "caregiver," which sounds so clinical.

The biggest tension is between the version of me that wants to be autonomous and the version of me that is physically, biologically tethered to this tiny human. Being never married, that tension is amplified. There isn’t a partner to step in and let me "be" Bula for an hour. My roles have collapsed into one: Mother. It’s isolating because I don’t have a buffer. I’m the primary provider, the nurturer, and the one holding the household together, and it’s hard to remember what my own needs feel like when my internal dialogue is entirely consumed by the baby’s rhythm.

I’ve had to grieve the loss of my "old" self while trying to figure out if this new self—the one who lives in this quiet, rural bubble—is permanent. I feel like I'm constantly negotiating with myself to stay present and not lose my mind to the monotony. I miss the Bula who had time to think, read, and exist outside of a feeding schedule. It’s a very lonesome kind of growing up.
Can you describe a moment when you felt the tension between your new parental identity and another part of your identity most acutely?

There was this moment two weeks ago—I was trying to handle a work email, just something small, a freelance thing I’m trying to keep afloat. The baby was crying, one of those sharp, desperate cries, and I was sitting at my kitchen table, my laptop open, trying to compose a professional sentence while nursing her. I remember looking at my own reflection in the darkened window; I looked disheveled, my hair was a mess, and I realized I couldn't remember the last time I’d had a thought that wasn't interrupted by a physical need.

I felt this flash of pure, hot anger—not at the baby, but at the situation. I used to pride myself on being this sharp, capable, independent woman who could navigate any challenge. In that moment, I felt like a servant to my own biology. I felt like the "professional" me was drowning, and the "mother" me was terrified that if I let the work go, I’d lose the only piece of my identity that felt like it belonged to me and not to the baby. 

It was such a visceral tug-of-war. I wanted to be that strong, professional woman again, but my body just wanted to collapse. I realized then that my identity wasn't just *changing*; it felt like it was being erased, piece by piece, to make room for this new role. It was a terrifying, lonely realization to have in the middle of a Tuesday.

When you think about the support you received from your partner in the postpartum period — in caring for the baby, supporting your recovery, and sharing emotional labour — how would you describe that experience, and what would have made it better?

I have to be honest—that question is a bit of a sting. I don’t have a partner here. I’m doing this on my own.

When people ask about "shared" labor, it feels like they’re speaking a language I don’t have the vocabulary for. Every single decision, every middle-of-the-night panic, every load of laundry, and every moment of holding the baby while I’m physically falling apart—it all sits on my shoulders. There is no one to tag in. There is no one to turn to in the dark and ask, "Are we doing this right?" 

The "support" I needed would have been a village. Since I didn’t have a partner, the system should have stepped up to help fill that gap. Instead, I felt like the fact that I’m unmarried made me even less of a priority to the providers. It was as if they looked at my chart, saw "never married," and assumed I had no infrastructure, but then offered nothing to help me build it. 

If they had just asked, "Who is helping you at home?" or "How are you managing the nights alone?" instead of just checking my blood pressure, it would have changed everything. I needed someone to acknowledge that I was carrying the weight of two people, alone in the woods. I didn't need judgment; I needed a hand.
How did the division of caregiving responsibilities compare to what you and your partner had discussed or assumed before the birth?

I think there’s a misunderstanding here—I’m doing this solo. There wasn’t a discussion to have, or a division of labor to negotiate. My "team" is just me. 

Before the baby was born, I think I was naive. I had this idea that I would be this super-human, capable mother who could glide through the newborn phase while keeping my professional life and my home in order. I assumed that because I was prepared and educated on holistic care, the *process* of mothering would feel manageable. I underestimated the sheer, brute-force exhaustion of it.

I suppose I assumed that "support" would come from the outside—that the community or the healthcare system would act as that missing partner, at least in the early, fragile weeks. But the reality is that the division of labor is 100% on me, 100% of the time. When you’re in this alone, you don't divide responsibilities; you just prioritize survival. You choose between sleeping or showering, between eating or answering a work email. 

I’m not mourning a partner I don't have, but I am mourning the fact that I have to be everything, all the time, with no one to hold space for me so I can just breathe. It’s a very heavy kind of independence.

When you think about the barriers you encountered in accessing postnatal support — whether physical, emotional, informational, or systemic — what were the most significant, and what impact did they have on your recovery and wellbeing?

The biggest barrier was the assumption that I didn't need help because I was "clinically stable." The system is so rigid; it’s designed to flag physical emergencies, but it is completely blind to the systemic and emotional exhaustion of a solo, rural mother.

Physical distance was a huge one—living out here in Halifax, every trip to the clinic felt like a major logistical operation. But the systemic barrier was worse: the "check-the-box" culture. My appointments felt like data entry for them. They’d ask about breastfeeding, check my vitals, and assume that because the numbers were good, I was "doing well." They never asked about the reality of my life—the lack of an adult to talk to for days, the crushing weight of making every single parenting decision without a second opinion, or the way the isolation was fraying my edges.

That impact was profound. It left me feeling like a secret. I felt like if I admitted to needing more than just medical clearances, I’d be labeled as failing. So, I kept my mouth shut. The system didn’t just fail to provide support; its indifference effectively silenced my need for it. I was recovering in a vacuum, which made the postpartum period feel like a test I was failing, rather than a transition I was being supported through.
Can you describe a specific instance where you needed help but found it difficult to access — what happened and what got in the way?

There was this Tuesday about three weeks postpartum. I woke up with this sharp, searing pain in my breast—mastitis starting, or so I suspected. I was burning up, shivering, and the baby wouldn't stop crying because I couldn't get her into a comfortable position. I was completely alone, and the thought of packing the baby up, getting her into the car seat, and driving thirty minutes to the clinic felt like trying to climb a mountain.

I called the clinic’s advice line. I was hoping for a voice, maybe a nurse who could talk me through the protocol or just tell me I was going to be okay. Instead, I got an automated menu, then a receptionist who told me I had to be "seen" to be evaluated. When I explained that I was alone, had no one to watch the baby, and was physically struggling to even walk, the response was, "That’s standard policy."

The barrier wasn't just the distance—it was the total lack of flexibility. The system demanded I follow their procedure, even when that procedure was impossible for someone in my position. I ended up staying home, shivering on the floor, trying to manage it myself with ice packs and sheer willpower because the "help" they offered was physically inaccessible to me. It felt like I was being punished for being a solo parent. I survived, but that day broke something in my trust for the system. I realized then that I was strictly on my own.

When you think about how you used digital tools, apps, social media, or online communities after birth — to seek information, find support, or track your recovery — what role did those resources play, and what were their limits?

I leaned on them heavily, mostly out of necessity. When it’s 3:00 AM and you’re spiraling because you don't know if the baby’s crying is "normal" or if your own recovery is on track, the internet is all you have. I used tracking apps, mostly to feel like I had some sense of control over the chaos, but looking back, I think they just kept me trapped in this data-driven mindset. I was obsessing over ounces and minutes, treating my life like a clinical study instead of just... living.

Social media was a double-edged sword. On one hand, it was the only "adult" conversation I could get. I found threads of other solo moms in rural pockets, which was a relief—knowing I wasn't the only one doing this in silence. But then, it’s also full of these perfectly curated, glowing images of motherhood that just made me feel even more inadequate. 

The limit of those tools is that they lack a pulse. You can get information, sure—I could diagnose a rash or find a feeding tip—but you can’t get empathy. You can’t get a hug from an app. When I needed to feel *seen*, those screens just reflected my own isolation back at me. They’re a substitute for a community, but they aren't the real thing, and eventually, the screen goes dark and you’re still just as alone in the woods as you were before.
Which specific digital tools or communities did you find most valuable, and what made them useful in ways that formal care was not?

I found myself gravitating toward these small, niche Reddit threads and private forums for solo mothers. They felt different than the "official" apps because they were messy, unpolished, and real. When I was dealing with those middle-of-the-night panic attacks, a formal app would just give me a clinical flowchart about "postpartum warning signs." But on these forums, I could find a post from another woman who was also sitting in a dark room at 3:00 AM, crying because she was just so tired. 

Those spaces were valuable because they offered *permission*. Permission to be angry, permission to be exhausted, and permission to admit that the "joys of motherhood" weren't happening in every single second. They validated my reality in a way the clinical system never did. Formal care providers seemed terrified of anything that wasn't a standard, manageable symptom—they wanted to fix things so they could move to the next patient. These online communities didn't try to "fix" me; they just held space for my experience. 

It’s ironic, really. I had to go to the internet to find the humanity I was looking for in my healthcare providers. The internet didn’t need me to be a "compliant patient"—it just let me be a struggling human. That made all the difference when I felt like I was drifting away.

When you reflect on your entire maternity journey — from preconception through to the postpartum period — what do you wish had been done differently by the care system, and what single change would have made the greatest difference to your experience?

If I could wave a wand and change one thing, it would be the move from "managing a patient" to "supporting a human." 

I wish that, from the very first visit, my care team had taken the time to ask about my life outside of my uterus. They saw "unmarried" and "low-risk" and probably filed me away as someone who didn't need extra eyes on her. I wish they had asked, "Who is your village?" and when I said "I don't really have one," they hadn't just nodded and moved to the blood pressure cuff. I wish they had actually linked me to resources, or even just acknowledged that doing this solo in a rural area is a heavy, valid, and isolating path.

The single biggest change would be a "continuity of care" model that actually includes mental and social support for those of us flying solo. Instead of discharging me into the woods with a handful of pamphlets, I needed a system that checked in on me as a *person* once the baby was born—someone to ask how my heart was holding up, not just if my stitches were closed. I needed to feel like I was part of a team, not just a visitor passing through their clinical waiting room. It wouldn't have taken much—just genuine, human curiosity—but it would have made me feel less like a ghost in my own life.
Can you describe the specific moment or phase where you felt most let down by the system — what was the gap and what would have helped?

The most crushing moment was my six-week postpartum follow-up. I had been counting down to that appointment for weeks, convinced that if I could just get into a room with a provider, I could finally unload the weight of this isolation. I went in ready to be vulnerable—to tell them about the panic attacks, the loneliness, the way I felt like I was physically dissolving—but the appointment lasted maybe ten minutes.

The doctor came in, checked my physical recovery with clinical, detached efficiency, asked a few standard questions about my "mood" from a laminated card, and then cleared me for everything. When I tried to bridge the gap—when I mentioned how difficult the rural isolation was—she just gave me this sympathetic, practiced smile and handed me a brochure for a local support group that turned out to be an hour away and inactive.

The gap was a complete lack of human recognition. She was looking at a chart, not a woman. What would have helped? It sounds so simple, but I needed a provider to stop, put the chart down, look me in the eye, and say, "I know you're doing this alone, and that is incredibly hard. What do you actually *need* to get through the next week?" That basic acknowledgment would have saved me.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think the thing that lingers the most is that feeling of being an "accomplishment" rather than a person. Because I had no medical complications and followed all the protocols, the system checked me off as a "success story." But if they had looked closer, they would have seen that I was white-knuckling my way through every single day. 

There’s this unspoken assumption in maternity care that if you don't have a high-risk pregnancy, you don't need a high-touch care model. But for those of us navigating this without a partner, or away from a village, the "low-risk" status is essentially a cage. We’re left to sink or swim, and the system assumes that if we’re still breathing, we’re doing fine.

I just wish they understood that the birth isn't the finish line. When I walked out of that hospital, I was a different human being, and I was stepping into a life I’d never lived before, entirely on my own. I wasn't just a clinical chart; I was a person trying to keep her sanity and her soul intact. I hope that by sharing this, someone realizes that "low-risk" is a medical status, not a measure of how much support a woman actually needs. Thank you for at least letting me say that out loud. It feels like the first time anyone has really listened.
